bytes.topic: Compute Models — VMs vs containers vs serverless vs edge, choosing the right one
bytes.title: GCP Compute Models — Picking the Right Primitive for Your Workload
bytes.body:
GCP offers four distinct compute primitives, and choosing wrong costs you money, latency, or operational pain.

**Compute Engine (VMs):** Use when you need full OS control, persistent GPU/TPU access, or workloads that don't containerize cleanly. Choose N2D for cost, C3 for compute-intensive, A100/H100 for ML training.

**GKE (Containers):** Right choice for long-running services needing horizontal scaling, multi-tenancy, or complex networking. Autopilot mode eliminates node management — bin-packing is handled by Google. Use Workload Identity instead of service account keys.

**Cloud Run (Serverless Containers):** Best for HTTP-triggered services with variable traffic. Scales to zero, billed per 100ms. Set `--concurrency` and `--max-instances` to prevent cold-start cascades under burst traffic. Supports gRPC and WebSockets since 2023.

**Cloud Run Functions (formerly Cloud Functions):** Event-driven, single-purpose handlers — Pub/Sub consumers, GCS triggers, Eventarc pipelines. Gen 2 runs on Cloud Run infrastructure, giving you up to 60-min timeout and 16GB RAM.

**Edge (Cloud CDN + Media CDN):** Not a compute layer — push static assets and cacheable API responses to Google's PoPs. Pair with Cloud Armor for WAF at the edge.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Deploy to Cloud Run with concurrency + scaling limits
gcloud run deploy my-service \
  --image gcr.io/my-project/my-service:latest \
  --region us-central1 \
  --platform managed \
  --concurrency 80 \
  --min-instances 1 \
  --max-instances 20 \
  --memory 512Mi \
  --cpu 1 \
  --no-allow-unauthenticated

bytes.topic: Networking — VPCs, subnets, security groups, load balancers, DNS
bytes.title: GCP Networking — Why Your VPC Is Global But Your Subnets Aren't
bytes.body:
GCP VPCs are global resources — a single VPC spans all regions, unlike AWS where VPCs are region-scoped. Subnets are regional, so one VPC can have a subnet in us-central1 and another in europe-west1, communicating over Google's private backbone with no peering required.

Firewall rules are VPC-scoped, not subnet-scoped, and they target VMs via network tags or service accounts — not security group IDs. A rule allowing tcp:8080 from tag `frontend` to tag `backend` applies across every subnet in the VPC, in every region.

For Private Google Access, enable it per-subnet so VMs without external IPs can reach Google APIs (BigQuery, GCS, Pub/Sub) via private.googleapis.com. Without it, those VMs need Cloud NAT or a public IP to egress.

Cloud DNS private zones are VPC-bound. Cross-project resolution requires DNS peering (not VPC peering) — a common footgun. Always use custom-mode VPCs in production; auto-mode creates a /20 in every region automatically, which collides with on-prem CIDR ranges fast.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Never use auto-mode in prod — subnets in every region by default
gcloud compute networks create prod-vpc \
  --subnet-mode=custom \
  --bgp-routing-mode=global

# Regional subnet with Private Google Access enabled
gcloud compute networks subnets create prod-subnet-us \
  --network=prod-vpc \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --range=10.0.0.0/20 \
  --enable-private-ip-google-access

# Firewall rule scoped by network tag, not subnet
gcloud compute firewall-rules create allow-backend \
  --network=prod-vpc \
  --allow=tcp:8080 \
  --source-tags=frontend \
  --target-tags=backend

bytes.topic: Storage — object vs block vs file storage, when to use each
bytes.title: GCP Storage — Object vs Block vs File: Picking the Right One
bytes.body: GCP gives you three storage primitives and conflating them is expensive. Cloud Storage (GCS) is object storage — globally durable, infinitely scalable, accessed over HTTP. Use it for assets, backups, data lake raw zones, and ML training datasets. It has no filesystem semantics; you GET/PUT by key. Persistent Disk (PD) and Hyperdisk are block storage — low-latency SSD volumes attached to Compute Engine or GKE nodes. Use them for databases (Postgres, MySQL), stateful containers, and OS boot volumes. Hyperdisk Extreme hits 350,000 IOPS for latency-sensitive workloads. Filestore is managed NFS — a fully POSIX-compliant shared filesystem. Use it when multiple GKE pods or VMs need concurrent read/write access to the same directory tree (e.g., shared ML checkpoints, CMS media mounts). The decision tree: serving static bytes to the internet → GCS; running a database on a VM → PD/Hyperdisk; shared mounts across multiple nodes → Filestore. Mixing them up (e.g., using Filestore for cold backups) is wasteful — GCS at Nearline tier costs ~70% less per GB.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Object: upload to GCS bucket
gsutil cp model_weights.bin gs://my-ml-bucket/checkpoints/

# Block: create a Hyperdisk Extreme PD and attach to a VM
gcloud compute disks create db-volume \
  --type=hyperdisk-extreme \
  --size=500GB \
  --provisioned-iops=100000 \
  --zone=us-central1-a

gcloud compute instances attach-disk my-db-vm \
  --disk=db-volume --zone=us-central1-a

# File: create a Filestore instance for shared NFS mount
gcloud filestore instances create shared-fs \
  --tier=BASIC_SSD --file-share=name=data,capacity=1TB \
  --network=name=default --zone=us-central1-a

bytes.topic: Databases as a Service — managed DB offerings, read replicas, automated backups
bytes.title: GCP Cloud SQL — Managed Databases, Read Replicas, and Automated Backups
bytes.body: Cloud SQL is GCP's fully managed relational database service supporting PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server. Instances run on Compute Engine under the hood, but Google handles patching, replication, and storage scaling. For read-heavy workloads, Cloud SQL supports cross-region read replicas — promoted to primary in under 60 seconds during failover. Replicas are created via `gcloud sql instances create` with `--master-instance-name` and `--replica-type=READ`. Point-in-time recovery (PITR) uses binary logs (MySQL) or WAL (PostgreSQL) to restore to any second within the retention window. Automated backups run on a configurable schedule and are stored in Cloud Storage, billed separately from instance storage. For high availability, enable the HA configuration which provisions a standby in a different zone with synchronous replication — failover is automatic and typically completes in under 35 seconds. Cloud SQL Auth Proxy (`cloud-sql-proxy`) handles IAM-based mTLS connections without exposing public IPs, replacing password-based access in production.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a PostgreSQL Cloud SQL instance with HA and automated backups
gcloud sql instances create prod-db \
  --database-version=POSTGRES_15 \
  --tier=db-custom-4-16384 \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --availability-type=REGIONAL \
  --backup-start-time=03:00 \
  --retained-backups-count=14 \
  --enable-point-in-time-recovery

# Add a cross-region read replica
gcloud sql instances create prod-db-replica \
  --master-instance-name=prod-db \
  --replica-type=READ \
  --region=us-east1

bytes.topic: IAM & Permissions — least privilege, roles vs policies, service accounts, never root
bytes.title: GCP IAM — Least Privilege with Service Accounts and Predefined Roles
bytes.body:
Every workload on GCP should run as a dedicated service account, never as a user account and never as the default compute service account (which has Editor on the entire project). Create purpose-built service accounts with the minimum predefined role needed — or custom roles if predefined ones are too broad.

Binding happens at the resource level, not just the project level. Attach roles to a Cloud Run service, a GCS bucket, or a Pub/Sub topic directly using `gcloud ... add-iam-policy-binding` with `--member=serviceAccount:...`. This scopes blast radius: a compromised Cloud Run SA can't touch your BigQuery datasets if the binding was never made.

Avoid primitive roles (`roles/owner`, `roles/editor`, `roles/viewer`) entirely — they're legacy and overly broad. Prefer `roles/run.invoker`, `roles/storage.objectViewer`, `roles/pubsub.publisher`, etc. Use `gcloud iam roles describe` to audit permissions before assigning. Rotate keys aggressively or, better, use Workload Identity Federation to eliminate key files altogether.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a least-privilege service account for a Cloud Run job
gcloud iam service-accounts create etl-job-sa \
  --display-name="ETL Job Service Account" \
  --project=my-project

# Grant only the permissions it needs — at the bucket level, not project
gcloud storage buckets add-iam-policy-binding gs://my-data-bucket \
  --member="serviceAccount:etl-job-sa@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
  --role="roles/storage.objectViewer"

# Bind the SA to the Cloud Run service (not the default compute SA)
gcloud run services update etl-service \
  --service-account=etl-job-sa@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com \
  --region=us-central1

bytes.topic: Cost Optimization — reserved vs spot vs on-demand, rightsizing, budget alerts
bytes.title: GCP Cost Optimization — Reserved, Spot, and On-Demand VMs with Budget Guardrails
bytes.body:
GCP's three compute pricing tiers serve distinct workloads. On-demand (standard) VMs have no commitment but the highest per-hour rate. Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) — GCP's equivalent of reserved instances — offer 1- or 3-year commitments for up to 57% savings on N2/C2 families; purchase them via `gcloud compute commitments create`. Spot VMs (formerly preemptible) cut costs by up to 91% but can be reclaimed with a 30-second ACPI signal — ideal for batch jobs, Dataflow pipelines, and GKE node pools with `--spot` flag.

Rightsizing: Cloud Monitoring's Recommender API surfaces machine-type recommendations automatically. Run `gcloud recommender recommendations list --recommender=google.compute.instance.MachineTypeRecommender` to pull idle or over-provisioned instances.

Budget alerts: create programmatic alerts via the Billing API so spikes page your team before the invoice lands. Pair with a `billingAccounts.budgets.create` call to set threshold rules at 50%, 90%, and 100% of forecast.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Pull rightsizing recommendations for a project
gcloud recommender recommendations list \
  --project=my-project \
  --location=us-central1-a \
  --recommender=google.compute.instance.MachineTypeRecommender \
  --format="table(name,stateInfo.state,primaryImpact.costProjection.cost.units)"

# Create a billing budget alert at 90% of $500/month
gcloud billing budgets create \
  --billing-account=ABCD-1234-EFGH \
  --display-name="prod-budget" \
  --budget-amount=500USD \
  --threshold-rule=percent=0.9,basis=CURRENT_SPEND \
  --all-projects

bytes.topic: High Availability — multi-AZ deployments, failover, provider SLAs
bytes.title: GCP High Availability — Multi-Region Failover with Global Load Balancing
bytes.body:
GCP's 99.99% SLA for Cloud Load Balancing applies only when you deploy across multiple regions — single-region setups get no SLA guarantee. Use a Global External Application Load Balancer with backend services spanning at least two regions. Traffic is routed via Anycast, so the nearest healthy region handles requests automatically with no DNS TTL delays during failover.

For Compute Engine, deploy managed instance groups (MIGs) in separate zones per region and set `failoverPolicy.failoverRatio` to control when cross-region spillover triggers. For Cloud SQL, enable high-availability (synchronous replication within a zone pair) and add a cross-region read replica promoted manually — or use AlloyDB which supports automatic failover in under 60 seconds.

For GKE, use multi-cluster Ingress with Fleet to distribute traffic across regional clusters. Health checks on the load balancer must use `/healthz` with a 2s timeout and 2 consecutive failures to trigger failover quickly without flapping. Always set `connectionDraining.drainingTimeoutSec` to 30s to avoid dropped in-flight requests during instance removal.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a global backend service with multi-region failover
gcloud compute backend-services create my-backend \
  --global \
  --protocol=HTTP \
  --health-checks=my-http-check \
  --connection-draining-timeout=30

# Add primary region backend (us-central1 MIG)
gcloud compute backend-services add-backend my-backend \
  --global \
  --instance-group=mig-us-central1 \
  --instance-group-region=us-central1 \
  --balancing-mode=UTILIZATION \
  --max-utilization=0.8

# Add failover region backend (us-east1 MIG)
gcloud compute backend-services add-backend my-backend \
  --global \
  --instance-group=mig-us-east1 \
  --instance-group-region=us-east1 \
  --balancing-mode=UTILIZATION \
  --max-utilization=0.8

bytes.topic: CDN & Edge — cache setup, invalidation, edge caching strategy
bytes.title: GCP Cloud CDN — Cache Setup, Invalidation, and Edge Strategy
bytes.body:
GCP Cloud CDN sits in front of Cloud Load Balancing backends (GCS buckets, NEGs, instance groups). Enable it per backend service with `--enable-cdn` and configure cache mode via `--cache-mode=CACHE_ALL_STATIC` (default) or `FORCE_CACHE_ALL` to cache even responses missing `Cache-Control` headers. Set `--default-ttl`, `--max-ttl`, and `--client-ttl` to control edge TTLs independently of origin headers.

For cache keys, use `--cache-key-policy` to include or exclude query strings, headers, and named cookies — critical when serving personalized or A/B-tested content. Cache invalidation is URL-pattern-based via `gcloud compute url-maps invalidate-cdn-cache`. Wildcard patterns like `/assets/*` flush entire paths without touching unrelated content.

For edge caching strategy: serve static assets from GCS with `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` and use content-hashed filenames. For dynamic API responses, set `Vary: Accept-Encoding, Authorization` carefully — CDN respects `Vary` and splits cache entries per value, which can destroy hit rates if Authorization is included. Use signed URLs or signed cookies for private content at the edge instead.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Enable Cloud CDN on an existing backend service
gcloud compute backend-services update my-backend \
  --global \
  --enable-cdn \
  --cache-mode=CACHE_ALL_STATIC \
  --default-ttl=3600 \
  --max-ttl=86400 \
  --client-ttl=600 \
  --cache-key-policy-include-host \
  --cache-key-policy-include-protocol

# Invalidate a path pattern across all edge POPs
gcloud compute url-maps invalidate-cdn-cache my-url-map \
  --path "/assets/*" \
  --global

bytes.topic: Serverless — cold starts, timeout limits, stateless design constraints
bytes.title: GCP Cloud Run & Cloud Functions — Surviving Cold Starts and Timeout Walls
bytes.body: Cloud Run instances cold-start when no warm container exists — typically 1–4s for Node.js, 3–8s for JVM. Minimum instances (--min-instances) eliminate cold starts at the cost of idle billing. Cloud Functions Gen2 (backed by Cloud Run) inherits the same model. Timeout limits are hard walls: Cloud Run caps at 3600s per request, Cloud Functions Gen2 at 3600s, but Gen1 HTTP functions cap at 540s — critical for long-running data pipelines. Beyond the wall, the request is killed without a response. Stateless design isn't optional — each container instance can serve concurrent requests, and filesystem writes to /tmp are instance-local, ephemeral, and capped at 512MB (configurable up to 32GB on Cloud Run). Use Cloud Storage for shared state, Firestore or Cloud Spanner for transactional state, and Memorystore (Redis) for session/cache. Avoid in-memory singletons that assume request affinity — Cloud Run's concurrency model (up to 1000 req/instance) means multiple requests share the same process simultaneously, not sequentially.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Deploy Cloud Run with min instances and extended timeout
gcloud run deploy my-service \
  --image gcr.io/my-project/my-service:latest \
  --min-instances 2 \
  --max-instances 100 \
  --concurrency 80 \
  --timeout 3600 \
  --memory 512Mi \
  --region us-central1 \
  --set-env-vars="INSTANCE_CONNECTION_NAME=my-project:us-central1:my-db"

# Check cold start behavior via Cloud Logging
gcloud logging read 'resource.type="cloud_run_revision" AND textPayload:"Cold Start"' \
  --limit 20 --format json

bytes.topic: Edge Computing — Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge, latency benefits
bytes.title: GCP Cloud Run + Cloudflare Workers — Slash Latency with Edge Routing
bytes.body: Cloud Run is regional. A service deployed to us-central1 adds 150–300ms RTT for users in Europe or Asia-Pacific — before your code even runs. Cloudflare Workers execute at 300+ PoPs globally, intercepting requests within ~5ms of the user. The pattern: route auth checks, geo-detection, A/B flag evaluation, and cache reads through a Worker, then proxy only cache-misses or authenticated requests to your Cloud Run origin URL. This removes entire round-trips for cacheable responses and offloads JWT verification (using Clerk or Firebase Auth public keys) from your Cloud Run instances. Workers also let you rewrite paths before they hit your Cloud Run service, enabling blue/green traffic splits without touching your GCP Load Balancer config or backend service weights. For latency-sensitive APIs backed by Cloud Spanner or Firestore, this edge layer cuts median P50 latency by 40–60% for globally distributed users — without replicating your Cloud Run services across regions.

bytes.topic: Managed Services — when managed beats self-hosted (Kafka, Redis, Postgres)
bytes.title: GCP Managed Services — When Managed Beats Self-Hosted for Kafka, Redis, and Postgres
bytes.body:
GCP offers three native managed alternatives that consistently outperform self-hosted setups on Compute Engine.

**Managed Service for Apache Kafka** handles broker scaling, rack-awareness, and rolling KRaft upgrades automatically. Self-hosting on GCE means owning quorum health, replication factor tuning, and disk I/O saturation—typically 20+ ops hours per month.

**Memorystore for Redis** delivers automatic failover via Sentinel (Standard tier) or Cluster mode with sub-minute RTO. It runs private-IP-only inside your VPC, integrates with Cloud IAM AUTH tokens, and patches the Redis binary without manual maintenance windows.

**Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL** (or AlloyDB for HTAP workloads) handles PITR, automated backups to GCS, read replicas, and IAM database authentication out of the box. AlloyDB's adaptive columnar engine delivers up to 100× analytics speedup over vanilla Postgres without changing a single query.

The crossover point: choose managed when operational burden exceeds the cost delta. On GCP that's almost always day one for Redis and Postgres. Kafka is the exception only above ~10 TB/day ingestion, where Pub/Sub unit pricing becomes more competitive than the Managed Kafka cluster cost.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Provision all three managed services via gcloud

# Managed Apache Kafka cluster (us-central1)
gcloud managed-kafka clusters create prod-kafka \
  --location=us-central1 \
  --cpu=3 --memory=3GiB

# Memorystore Redis 7 — Standard HA tier
gcloud redis instances create prod-redis \
  --region=us-central1 --tier=STANDARD_HA \
  --redis-version=redis_7_0 --size=5

# Cloud SQL Postgres 16 — regional HA
gcloud sql instances create prod-pg \
  --database-version=POSTGRES_16 \
  --tier=db-custom-4-15360 \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --availability-type=REGIONAL \
  --database-flags=cloudsql.iam_authentication=on

bytes.topic: Security — encryption at rest/transit, secrets manager, WAF, DDoS protection
bytes.title: GCP Security Hardening — Encryption, Secrets, WAF, and DDoS in One Stack
bytes.body: GCP encrypts all data at rest by default using AES-256 via Google's Key Management Service. For tighter control, use Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) with Cloud KMS — attach a key ring to Cloud SQL, GCS, or BigQuery at resource creation time. In transit, GCP enforces TLS 1.2+ on all managed services; for internal service mesh traffic, use Anthos Service Mesh with mTLS enforced via `PeerAuthentication` policy set to `STRICT`. Secrets belong in Secret Manager, not env vars — grant access with IAM binding `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` scoped to a specific service account, not the default compute SA. For WAF, Cloud Armor security policies attach directly to backend services via the load balancer; use preconfigured ModSecurity rules (OWASP CRS) with `evaluatePreconfiguredExpr('sqli-v33-stable')`. DDoS protection is layered: Google Front End absorbs volumetric attacks globally, while Cloud Armor adaptive protection detects L7 anomalies and auto-suggests rules. Enable it per backend service with `--enable-layer7-ddos-defense`.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Attach a Cloud KMS CMEK to a new Cloud SQL instance
gcloud sql instances create prod-db \
  --database-version=POSTGRES_15 \
  --disk-encryption-key=projects/my-project/locations/us-central1/keyRings/prod-ring/cryptoKeys/sql-key

# Grant Secret Manager access to a specific service account
gcloud secrets add-iam-policy-binding db-password \
  --member="serviceAccount:api-sa@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
  --role="roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor"

# Enable Cloud Armor adaptive DDoS protection on a backend service
gcloud compute backend-services update my-backend-service \
  --security-policy=prod-armor-policy \
  --global

bytes.topic: PaaS vs IaaS — Railway/Vercel/Fly.io vs raw AWS/GCP/Azure, DX vs control
bytes.title: GCP PaaS vs IaaS — Cloud Run vs GKE vs Railway/Fly.io: Where to Draw the Control Line
bytes.body:
GCP gives you a native PaaS-to-IaaS spectrum before you ever touch Railway or Fly.io. Cloud Run sits at the managed end — push a container, get HTTPS, autoscaling, and IAM-integrated service accounts in seconds, with zero node management. GKE Autopilot sits in the middle: Kubernetes semantics, but Google manages the control plane and node pools. Raw GKE Standard or Compute Engine puts you in full IaaS territory — you own OS patching, networking, and cluster upgrades.

External PaaS like Railway and Fly.io abstract all of this away, but at a cost: you lose VPC-native connectivity to Cloud SQL private IPs, Workload Identity Federation for keyless auth, and fine-grained IAM roles. If your service talks to Memorystore, Pub/Sub, or Vertex AI, staying inside GCP's network boundary eliminates auth complexity and egress charges. Cloud Run with `--vpc-connector` or Direct VPC Egress gives you Railway-level DX while keeping private service connectivity intact. Default to Cloud Run unless you need stateful workloads, custom kernel params, or multi-container pods — then step up to GKE Autopilot before reaching for raw IaaS.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Cloud Run deploy — PaaS-level DX, GCP-native control
gcloud run deploy my-service \
  --image us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-project/repo/my-service:latest \
  --region us-central1 \
  --network my-vpc \
  --subnet my-subnet \
  --vpc-egress all-traffic \
  --service-account my-service@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com \
  --set-env-vars DB_HOST=10.0.0.3 \
  --no-allow-unauthenticated

bytes.topic: Region Selection — latency, data residency, compliance requirements
bytes.title: GCP Region Selection — Latency, Data Residency, and Compliance
bytes.body: Picking the wrong GCP region costs you in latency, compliance fines, or both. Start with latency: use `gcloud compute routes list` and GCP's public latency dashboard to benchmark round-trips from your users to candidate regions. For EU users, `europe-west1` (Belgium) and `europe-west4` (Netherlands) consistently outperform `us-central1` by 80–120ms. Data residency is enforced via Organization Policies — set `constraints/gcp.resourceLocations` in your `orgpolicy.yaml` to restrict resource creation to specific locations. For compliance, GDPR workloads must stay in EU regions; HIPAA requires a signed BAA with Google, achievable in any region but typically scoped to `us-central1` or `us-east1` in practice. Use VPC Service Controls with Access Context Manager to enforce perimeter boundaries at the region level. Multi-region Cloud Spanner (`nam6`, `eur3`) gives you high availability without sacrificing residency guarantees. Always check the GCP Products by Region matrix before committing — not all services (e.g., Vertex AI features, Memorystore tiers) are available in every region.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Restrict all resources to EU regions via Organization Policy
cat > org_policy.yaml <<EOF
name: projects/MY_PROJECT_ID/policies/gcp.resourceLocations
spec:
  rules:
  - values:
      allowedValues:
      - in:eu-locations
EOF

gcloud org-policies set-policy org_policy.yaml \
  --project=MY_PROJECT_ID

# Verify effective policy
gcloud org-policies describe gcp.resourceLocations \
  --project=MY_PROJECT_ID

bytes.topic: Compliance — SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA considerations per provider
bytes.title: GCP Compliance — SOC2, GDPR, and HIPAA Without the Guesswork
bytes.body: GCP provides built-in compliance controls, but wiring them correctly is your responsibility. For SOC2, enable Cloud Audit Logs (Data Access logs are off by default — enable them per resource via `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` + Org Policy). Use Access Transparency logs if you're on Enterprise tier to audit Google's own access. For GDPR, leverage Data Residency Org Policies (`constraints/gcp.resourceLocations`) to pin resources to EU regions, and use CMEK (Cloud KMS) so you control encryption keys — critical for right-to-erasure: delete the key version, data becomes unreadable. Configure VPC Service Controls to create a data perimeter around BigQuery/GCS, blocking exfiltration even from compromised service accounts. For HIPAA, GCP signs a BAA — but only covered services qualify (Cloud SQL, GKE, BigQuery, etc. — check the HIPAA implementation guide). Enable DLP API to auto-detect and de-identify PHI in GCS and BigQuery before it hits downstream pipelines. Use `CMEK + Cloud HSM` for hardware-backed key management to satisfy HIPAA encryption-at-rest requirements.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Lock resources to EU regions (GDPR data residency)
gcloud resource-manager org-policies set-policy \
  --organization=ORG_ID \
  gdpr-location-policy.yaml

# gdpr-location-policy.yaml
# constraint: constraints/gcp.resourceLocations
# listPolicy:
#   allowedValues:
#     - in:eu-locations

# Enable Data Access audit logs for Cloud Storage (SOC2)
gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format=json > policy.json
# Add under auditConfigs: service: storage.googleapis.com, logTypes: [DATA_READ, DATA_WRITE]
gcloud projects set-iam-policy PROJECT_ID policy.json

# Scan GCS bucket for PHI (HIPAA)
gcloud dlp jobs create --project=PROJECT_ID \
  --inspect-template=projects/PROJECT_ID/inspectTemplates/hipaa-template \
  --storage-config='{"cloudStorageOptions":{"fileSet":{"url":"gs://your-bucket/**"}}}'

bytes.topic: Deployment Models — blue/green, canary, rolling updates on cloud infra
bytes.title: GCP Deployment Models — Blue/Green, Canary, and Rolling Updates on Cloud Run & GKE
bytes.body:
GCP offers first-class support for all three strategies, but the right tool depends on your runtime. On Cloud Run, canary deployments are native: deploy a new revision with --no-traffic, then use --to-revisions to split a percentage. No load balancer config needed. Blue/green is just a traffic flip — shift 0% → 100% to the new revision after validation, with instant rollback via a single gcloud command.

On GKE, rolling updates are controlled by strategy.rollingUpdate in your Deployment spec. Tune maxSurge and maxUnavailable to balance speed vs. availability. Combine with PodDisruptionBudgets to guard against simultaneous node and pod churn during cluster upgrades. True blue/green on GKE means two Deployments + a Service selector swap, or weighted NEG backends behind a Cloud Load Balancing BackendConfig.

Cloud Deploy abstracts all three into a managed delivery pipeline. Define serialPipeline.stages in clouddeploy.yaml, target Cloud Run or GKE clusters, and get canaryDeployment percentage steps, verifyJob hooks, and approval gates with full Audit Log coverage — no custom rollout scripts needed.
bytes.code_snippet:
# 1. Deploy new Cloud Run revision — zero traffic
gcloud run deploy my-service \
  --image gcr.io/my-project/my-service:v2 \
  --no-traffic \
  --region us-central1

# 2. Canary: send 10% to new revision
gcloud run services update-traffic my-service \
  --to-revisions my-service-00002-xyz=10,my-service-00001-abc=90 \
  --region us-central1

# 3. Full promotion after validation
gcloud run services update-traffic my-service \
  --to-latest \
  --region us-central1

bytes.topic: Multi-cloud & Vendor Lock-in — portability trade-offs, abstraction layers
bytes.title: GCP Vendor Lock-in — Where Portability Dies and Where It Survives
bytes.body:
GCP's managed services sit on a portability spectrum. Cloud Run containers and GKE workloads are genuinely portable — your image runs on any OCI-compliant runtime. Cloud Storage supports S3-compatible HMAC auth, making it interchangeable at the SDK level. But go one layer deeper and the abstraction breaks: `google_spanner_instance` in Terraform abstracts syntax, not semantics — there's no AWS equivalent to point the same config at.

The hard lock-in lives in analytics and ML: BigQuery's columnar engine, Dataflow jobs wired to Pub/Sub via `apache_beam.io.ReadFromPubSub`, and Vertex AI Feature Store have no drop-in cross-cloud replacements. Abstracting these behind a repository interface helps unit testing but won't survive a cloud migration.

Anthos extends GKE's control plane to AWS and on-prem, but management still routes through GCP — it's operational portability, not financial independence. For auth, prefer Workload Identity Federation over service account keys; OIDC tokens are cloud-portable, JSON key files are not.

Pragmatic rule: accept lock-in on BigQuery and Pub/Sub where GCP's operational cost justifies it, keep compute on Cloud Run, and document every `google_*` Terraform resource as a migration cost line item.

bytes.topic: Monitoring & Billing — cost anomaly detection, usage dashboards
bytes.title: GCP Cost Anomaly Detection — Catch Billing Spikes Before They Hit Your Invoice
bytes.body:
GCP's Cloud Billing anomaly detection uses ML to alert you when spend deviates unexpectedly from your baseline — no threshold config required. Enable it under Billing → Budget & alerts → Anomaly detection. For programmatic control, use the Cloud Billing Budget API to create budgets with `thresholdRules` and Pub/Sub notifications, then trigger Cloud Functions to auto-disable APIs or cap quotas on breach.

For dashboards, Cloud Monitoring's Metrics Explorer exposes `billing/bytes_downloaded`, `compute.googleapis.com/instance/uptime`, and service-level cost metrics. Pair this with Looker Studio connected to the `cloud_billing_export` BigQuery dataset — enable both Standard Usage Cost and Detailed Usage Cost exports in Billing → Billing export. Query `gcp_cost_export.detailed_usage_cost` to break spend down by `resource.name`, `sku.description`, and `labels` for per-team or per-feature attribution. Set up scheduled queries to populate a `daily_cost_summary` table and alert via Cloud Monitoring when 7-day rolling spend exceeds budget thresholds.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a billing budget with Pub/Sub alert via gcloud
gcloud billing budgets create \
  --billing-account=BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID \
  --display-name="prod-anomaly-guard" \
  --budget-amount=5000USD \
  --threshold-rule=percent=0.5,basis=CURRENT_SPEND \
  --threshold-rule=percent=0.9,basis=CURRENT_SPEND \
  --threshold-rule=percent=1.0,basis=FORECASTED_SPEND \
  --notifications-rule-pubsub-topic=projects/MY_PROJECT/topics/billing-alerts \
  --filter-projects=projects/MY_PROJECT \
  --calendar-period=MONTH
